title: QUIC Interop Endpoint-Host Shell Architecture
purpose: Describe the thin endpoint-host shell that bridges the library-owned runtime endpoint through a real connected UDP socket without moving protocol ownership into the harness.
design_summary:
The endpoint-host shell keeps the harness on the adapter side of the boundary. It owns only the connected UDP socket lifecycle, receive loop, outbound datagram handoff, and optional observers used to see ingress, transition, and effect activity.

The library keeps the actual runtime ownership. `QuicConnectionRuntimeEndpoint` owns route registration and ingress dispatch, `QuicConnectionEndpointHost` bridges the connected socket to the runtime, `QuicConnectionRuntime` owns handshake and send/recovery behavior, and `QuicHandshakeFlowCoordinator` keeps packet open/protect helpers inside the library.
alternatives_considered: Move the UDP receive/send loop into the harness runner. Rejected because it would make the harness own transport logic instead of only the socket boundary.
Leave the harness as a pure 127-returning stub. Rejected because the repository now has enough library-owned seam to exercise a real socket/process boundary without faking support.
edge_cases_and_constraints: Route misses must be reported as unroutable and must not reach the runtime consumer.
Unsupported interop testcases must remain honest and keep returning `127` until the runner is enabled.
The shell must not claim handshake completion, 1-RTT data support, or key update support.
data_and_state_considerations: The shell keeps only connected-socket state, cancellation, and the observers needed to surface ingress and egress activity. Connection identity, packet protection material, handshake transcript progression, and sender/recovery state remain in the library-owned runtime.

supplemental_sections.heading: Scope
supplemental_sections.content: This slice establishes the smallest process/socket boundary needed to drive a single connection through the library-owned runtime. It does not claim full handshake enablement or general-purpose server/client hosting.

supplemental_sections.heading: Boundary Split
supplemental_sections.content:
Harness-owned code stays limited to `InteropEndpointHost.cs`, runner dispatch, environment parsing, and process lifecycle.
Library-owned code keeps `QuicConnectionEndpointHost`, `QuicConnectionRuntimeEndpoint`, `QuicConnectionRuntime`, and the packet open/protect helpers.

risks: A later runner integration could accidentally move protocol ownership back into the harness if the boundary is not kept narrow.
Handshake testcase enablement still needs runner-side wiring plus the remaining Initial/DCID bootstrap work.
The shell proves socket bridging, not a completed QUIC handshake.
